title: Survey of LLM Evaluation Frameworks in 2026
body:
This note surveys leading frameworks for evaluating large language models (LLMs) as of 2026. Core toolkits include: EleutherAI's lm-eval-harness for standardized academic benchmarks; Stanford CRFM's HELM 2.0 for holistic evaluation with coverage, fairness, and robustness slices; LightEval for fast batched inference and plug-in tasks; OpenAI Evals and promptfoo for programmatic eval orchestration and regression testing; and LangSmith, TruLens, and Arize Phoenix for production-grade LLM application evaluation.

Common metrics span exact match and F1 for QA, Rouge/BLEU for generation, model-judge preferences (pairwise wins, ELO), and automatic rubric-based scoring. Best practices: seed control, dataset version pinning, reproducible prompts, judge-bias mitigation via A/B order randomization, and transparent reporting. When choosing LLM evaluation frameworks, consider licensing, task coverage, and ease of integration. See also domain-specific suites like MMLU, BIG-bench, and SWE-bench.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation Benchmarks and Metrics
body:
RAG evaluation focuses on both retrieval and generation quality. Key metrics: context precision/recall (did retrieval fetch relevant evidence?), answer relevancy (does the response address the query?), and faithfulness/groundedness (is the answer supported by sources?). Frameworks like RAGAS, TruLens, LangSmith evals, and Arize-Phoenix provide ready-made evaluators and tracing.

Offline evals use held-out question-answer sets with gold documents; online evals use click data and A/B tests. Diagnostics to include: null-context baselines, retrieval latency, chunking strategy impact, and sensitivity to prompt changes.

title: Prompt Engineering Patterns for Robustness
body:
Robust prompt engineering patterns include chain-of-thought with self-consistency sampling, instruction-sandwiching, constrained decoding via JSON schemas, and tool-aware prompts with explicit input/output contracts. Reflection and critique loops (e.g., 'generate → critique → revise') can reduce hallucinations.

Guard against prompt injection by delimiting untrusted content and using verifier prompts. Track prompt versions and run automated evals after any change.

title: Dataset Splitting and Leakage Prevention
body:
To prevent train-test leakage in NLP, use document-level or group-aware splits so that near-duplicates do not straddle sets. Prefer chronological splits for time-evolving data. Deduplicate aggressively with hashing and locality-sensitive hashing.

Record dataset provenance and exact filtering rules. Validate splits by measuring n-gram overlap and nearest-neighbor distances between train and test. For RAG systems, ensure that evaluation questions do not appear in the indexed corpus.

title: Agent Safety Guardrails and Red-Teaming Notes
body:
Effective agent guardrails combine policy prompting, allow/deny lists for tools, input sanitization, and post-generation filters. Red-teaming should cover prompt injection, data exfiltration, jailbreaks, and social-engineering scenarios.

Use automated adversarial test suites and human review. Measure safety with refusal accuracy and rule coverage; log incidents for iterative improvement.
